summary: Cantrill reflects on the outsized influence of Brown University's CS169 operating systems course on Silicon Valley, noting that all three DTrace creators and seven key contributors to Solaris 10's revolutionary technologies (DTrace, ZFS, SMF, FMA, Zones) came from this single course taught by Tom Doeppner over thirty years. The post announces a symposium at Brown honoring DTrace and former CS169 TAs, where Cantrill presented on 'the inculcation of systems thinking.' He hints at deeper thoughts on systems education while sharing that his actual talk ranged from Soviet aircraft to Ruby stack traces.
key_points: All three DTrace creators came from Brown's CS169 operating systems course
Seven CS169 alumni at Sun played major roles in Solaris 10's key technologies
The course has produced top engineers at NetApp, SGI, Adobe, VMware and many startups
Tom Doeppner has taught CS169 for thirty years, creating a legendary program
Brown hosted a symposium honoring DTrace and CS169's contributions to industry
money_quotes: This course has been taught by the same professor, Tom Doeppner, over its thirty year lifetime, and has become something of a legend in Silicon Valley, having produced some of the top engineers at major companies like NetApp, SGI, Adobe, and VMware – not to mention tons of smaller companies.
At Sun, CS169 has cast a particularly long shadow, with seven CS169 alums having together played major roles in developing many of the revolutionary technologies in Solaris 10.
Seizing the opportunity for intellectual indulgence, I chose to reflect on a broad topic: the inculcation of systems thinking.
key_insight: A single rigorous operating systems course, taught consistently by one professor over decades, can produce an extraordinary concentration of talent that shapes major technologies across the industry.
